title: Thinking in Bets: Making Smarter Decisions When You Don't Have All the Facts
excerpt: Former World Series of Poker champion Annie Duke shows how treating decisions as bets — with probabilistic thinking, accountability for luck versus skill, and mental time travel — leads to dramatically better outcomes under uncertainty.
keyIdeas: Resulting — judging decision quality by outcome alone — is the single biggest obstacle to learning from experience
Treating every decision as a bet forces you to calibrate your confidence and acknowledge uncertainty
A 'decision journal' and a 'truth-seeking' group (like a poker podcast) counteract cognitive biases
Mental time travel — pre-commitment, pre-mortems, and backcasting — bridges the gap between short-term emotion and long-term judgment
keyTakeaways: Separate the quality of a decision from the quality of its outcome: a good decision can have a bad result
Use bet-framing to overcome ego and cognitive biases — ask yourself what odds you'd give someone else
Build a decision journal to track your reasoning, then review past calls to calibrate your judgment
Surround yourself with a peer group that rewards intellectual honesty over being 'right'
whoShouldRead: Anyone who makes consequential decisions under uncertainty — executives, investors, entrepreneurs
Poker players and gamblers looking to translate table skills into life skills
Behavioral science enthusiasts who enjoyed Thinking, Fast and Slow and want practical applications
People prone to agonizing over past decisions or overconfident about their judgment
whoShouldSkip: Readers seeking a purely academic treatment of decision theory
Anyone averse to poker analogies and uncomfortable with probabilistic language
